confidence.disclosure: Farid ud-Din Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Attar Nishapuri is one of the great Persian Sufi poets, but the historical record about his life is unusually thin: he is hardly mentioned in the Persian historical chronicles of his own time, and most of what is known about him is reconstructed from references in his own works and from much later hagiographical traditions. The major fixed points are his association with Nishapur, his profession as a pharmacist and physician (the meaning of 'Attar'), his composition of the Mantiq al-Tayr and the Tadhkirat al-Awliya, and the tradition (probably correct) that he died in the Mongol sack of Nishapur in 1221.

sources: Attar, Mantiq al-Tayr (translated as The Conference of the Birds)
Attar, Tadhkirat al-Awliya (Memoirs of the Saints)
Hellmut Ritter, Das Meer der Seele (1955)

journey.event: Born around 1145 in Nishapur in Khurasan (modern northeastern Iran). His father is a pharmacist, the trade that gives him his epithet Attar (the herbalist or perfumer). The exact birth year is uncertain; some sources place it as early as 1142.

Trains in his father's profession. As an attar he combines what would later be separated into pharmacy and medicine, preparing herbal remedies, perfumes, and treating patients. The shop becomes a meeting place for travellers and dervishes from across the Islamic east.

Inherits the family pharmacy and runs it for many years. Later tradition has him serving 500 patients a day. Through his shop he encounters the wandering Sufis of his time and is drawn deeply to the inner path.

Travels for some years through the eastern Islamic lands - reportedly visiting Iraq, Egypt, Syria, Mecca, India, and Turkestan, though the extent of his actual travels is uncertain. Whatever the geographical reality, his literary horizons certainly include the whole Islamic world.

Returns to Nishapur and devotes himself to his pharmacy and to writing. From this period date most of his great works: he composes them while serving customers in the shop, working on his manuscripts in moments between patients.

Composes his most famous work, the Mantiq al-Tayr (The Conference of the Birds), an allegorical mathnawi of about 4,500 lines. The birds of the world set out under the leadership of the hoopoe in search of their king, the Simorgh, and pass through seven valleys of the spiritual path before discovering that the Simorgh is themselves (si murgh = thirty birds in Persian).

Composes the Tadhkirat al-Awliya (Memoirs of the Saints), the only one of his major works in prose. It is a collection of biographies and sayings of 72 early Sufi masters, from al-Hasan al-Basri to al-Hallaj, and is the most influential work of Sufi hagiography in Persian.

Around 1207 (according to a tradition preserved by later writers, though doubted by many modern scholars) the small boy Jalal al-Din - later to be known as Rumi - passes through Nishapur with his father Baha al-Din Walad. Attar is said to have noticed the child and given him a copy of his Asrar-nama (Book of Secrets), saying 'This boy will set the hearts of the world on fire.'

Continues to live and work in Nishapur. By his own account in his later poems he composes more than 100,000 lines of verse over his lifetime; about a dozen mathnawis and a divan survive to us, while the authenticity of others remains disputed.

In April 1221 the Mongol army of Genghis Khan, under the command of his son-in-law Toghachar, sacks Nishapur. Toghachar himself is killed by an arrow during the assault, and the Mongols slaughter the entire population in revenge - reportedly piling up the heads of 1.7 million dead in pyramids outside the city. Attar, then about 76 years old, is killed in the massacre. His traditional tomb in Nishapur, the present mausoleum dating from the 16th century, is one of the major literary shrines of Iran.
